summary: Justin Brennan mentors Brian Norton's AIOS members through a detailed underwriting review of a 23-unit multifamily property in Cleveland, OH. The discussion covers critical deal-analysis fundamentals including property tax calculations, expense verification, unit-mix conversions, and the mathematical relationship between NOI and property valuation. While the specific Cleveland deal may not match Brian's target markets (GA/TN/AL), the underwriting principles and mental framework are directly applicable to his 15-50 unit value-add deals.
takeaways: Use ChatGPT to determine county-specific property tax assessment methods and rates rather than assuming broker numbers, which are often incorrect by $15,000+

Verify expense line items skeptically: for a 1926 building with 23 units, maintenance/repairs of only $1,800 is unrealistic; use formula of 23 units × 30% vacancy = 7 units × $1,500 = ~$10,000 as baseline
For stabilized properties with mixed government tenants (CHA/Eden), underwrite vacancy/credit loss at 5-7% rather than relying on OM data
Management fees of 5-6% are standard, but verify the property management company has proper systems, infrastructure, and team before accepting lower rates
For properties with minimal obvious renovations, budget $25,000-$50,000 capex reserve from day one rather than inflated amounts

When sending aggressive LOIs, call the broker immediately and say: 'It's based on numbers I'm looking at—I'd love to go over it with you and see where maybe I'm wrong' to position yourself as collaborative, not adversarial

Use the income approach to valuation: every $100/month rent increase per unit on a 10-unit property = $220,000 in added value at a 5.5% cap rate, demonstrating the power of operational improvements over market comps

skill_candidates.change: Implement skeptical verification of broker-provided expense assumptions, especially property taxes and maintenance line items
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin repeatedly points out broker errors in property tax projections (same rates for current and proforma despite different assessment methods) and unrealistic maintenance numbers ($1,800 on 1926 building = obviously low); provides calculation method: 23 units × 0.3 × $1,500 = ~$10,000 correct baseline

skill_candidates.change: Master the NOI-to-valuation math to evaluate rent increases and capital improvements ROI
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin demonstrates: $100/month rent increase × 10 units = $12,000/year income = $220,000 property value increase at 5.5% cap rate; then shows actual deal example where changing unit mix (19 1BR+4 2BR to 5 1BR+18 2BR) increases NOI from $117k to $191k, adding $1.2M value

skill_candidates.change: Use ChatGPT systematically to determine county-specific tax assessment rules rather than extrapolating from broker OM figures
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin explains different counties assess taxes differently (some at sale, some every 3-10 years, some based on percentages); shows how to prompt ChatGPT with property address and county to get accurate rate; notes that brokers typically use wrong methodology and create $15,000+ underwriting errors

title: Tuesday Mentorship Q&A - Mastering Multifamily Numbers Fast_12-02-25 | Justin Brennan
